1: A theme: Psalm 37 is a Wisdom acrostic — each Hebrew stanza begins with successive letters of the alphabet, encoding the completeness of the instruction within its formal structure. "Do not let evildoers upset you" (ʾal-titḥar bĕmĕrēʿîm) is the command that the entire psalm elaborates: covenant fidelity despite appearances. Christ embodies the answer to this command — the one who did not lose faith or stability despite the apparent triumph of his executioners, who trusted the divine outcome through the darkest crisis (Hebrews 5:7-9).
2: A theme: "For like grass they will soon be cut down; like green plants they will wither away." The grass-withering image recurs across the Psalter and Isaiah (Isaiah 40:6-8: "All flesh is grass... but the word of our God stands forever"). 1 Peter 1:24-25 quotes Isaiah 40 in the context of proclaiming the gospel: "but the word of the Lord endures forever — and this is the word that was proclaimed to you." The withering of the wicked is the backdrop against which Christ's resurrection-victory stands; his life does not wither.
3: A theme: "Trust in the LORD and do good; live in the land and enjoy secure provision." The triple covenant command — trust, do good, dwell — is the pattern of faithful land-tenure that the Deuteronomic framework promised. Christ is the one who perfectly "trusts in the LORD and does good" (Matthew 3:17: "This is my Son, whom I love; with him I am well pleased") — the validation of his total covenant fidelity. The "secure provision" (ûrʿēh ʾĕmûnāh, literally "pasture faithfulness/truth") is the New Covenant abundance Christ provides.
4: A revelation of God: "Find your delight in the LORD, and he will give you the desires of your heart." The divine responsiveness to delight — the promise that aligning one's desires with God produces fulfillment of those desires — is the covenant logic of John 15:7: "If you remain in me and my words remain in you, ask whatever you wish, and it will be done for you." Christ as the one who mediates this promise makes it available to his people: delighting in the Father through the Son produces prayer that is in alignment with the Father's will (1 John 5:14-15).
5: A theme: "Commit your way to the LORD; trust in him, and he will bring it to pass." The act of committing or rolling (gōl) one's path onto YHWH is the posture of total dependence. 1 Peter 5:7 quotes the Psalm's framework: "Cast all your anxiety on him because he cares for you." Christ models this total commitment most fully in Gethsemane: "Not my will, but yours be done" (Luke 22:42) — rolling the entire burden of the passion and its outcome onto the Father. The Father "brings it to pass" in the resurrection.
6: A theme: "He will bring your righteousness to light and your cause bright as noon." The divine vindication of the righteous person — their righteousness emerging from obscurity into noon-light — is the eschatological pattern of the resurrection. Romans 1:4 states that Christ "was designated the Son of God in power according to the Spirit of holiness by his resurrection from the dead" — the resurrection is the divine bringing-to-light of Christ's righteousness, the noon-brightness after the darkness of the cross.
7: A revelation of God: "Be still before the LORD and wait patiently for him." The discipline of stillness and patient waiting (hittḥōlēl lô) before divine action is the anti-urgency that runs against every instinct of the flesh. Hebrews 10:36: "You need to persevere so that when you have done the will of God, you will receive what he has promised. For, 'In just a little while, he who is coming will come and will not delay.'" The waiting posture of Psalm 37 is the posture Christ's people practice until his return — which the psalm itself anticipates (vv 9-11).
8: A revelation of God: "Let go of anger and abandon wrath; do not fret — it leads only to evil." The divine wisdom about emotional regulation in the face of injustice — the instruction not to let indignation produce reactive evil — is the countercultural logic of the Sermon on the Mount (Matthew 5:43-45: "Love your enemies and pray for those who persecute you"). Christ embodies this perfectly at the cross: 1 Peter 2:23, "When they hurled their insults at him, he did not retaliate; when he suffered, he made no threats. Instead, he entrusted himself to him who judges justly."
9: A direct connection: "For the wicked will be cut off, but those who wait for the LORD will inherit the earth (yîrĕšû-ʾāreṣ)." The "inherit the earth" (yāraš ʾereṣ) formula appears five times in Psalm 37 (vv 9, 11, 22, 29, 34). Jesus lifts verse 11's wording directly into the Beatitudes (Matthew 5:5), making this the bridge between the Psalm's Wisdom framework and the eschatological Kingdom. Those who "wait for the LORD" in Psalm 37's terms are those who trust in the Messiah — Christ is both the one waited for and the one through whom the inheritance comes.
10: A theme: "In just a little while the wicked will be gone; you will search for his place, but it will not be found." The sudden and total disappearance of the wicked — no trace remaining — is the Psalm's idiom for divine judgment. The NT uses this language eschatologically: Revelation 20:11 ("the earth and the heavens fled from his presence, and there was no place for them") and Psalms 21:4 ("the old order of things has passed away"). The wicked of Psalms 37:10 foreshadow the final disappearance of everything opposed to the reign of Christ.
11: A direct citation: "But the meek will inherit the earth and delight in abundant peace." Jesus quotes this verse verbatim in the third Beatitude (Matthew 5:5: "Blessed are the meek, for they will inherit the earth"). This is one of the clearest cases of Jesus placing himself within the Psalter's eschatological framework: the "meek" of Psalm 37 who wait for YHWH's vindication are the "meek" of the Beatitudes who receive the Kingdom. Christ himself is the paradigmatic meek one — the one who "is gentle and humble in heart" (Matthew 11:29) and who inherits the earth through resurrection and ascension (Ephesians 1:22).
12: A shadow: "The wicked man schemes against the righteous and grinds his teeth at him." The image of the wicked plotting and raging against the righteous is fulfilled most dramatically in the conspiracy against Jesus: Matthew 26:3-4 ("the chief priests and the elders of the people assembled in the palace of the high priest... and they schemed to arrest Jesus secretly and kill him"). The "grinding of teeth" at the righteous is the visceral hatred that the Passion narratives document — the opposition to the one who embodies righteousness.
13: A revelation of God: "The Lord laughs at the wicked, for he sees that their day is coming." The divine laugh at the schemes of those who oppose his anointed (cf. Psalms 2:4: "He who sits in the heavens laughs; the Lord scoffs at them") is the perspective of absolute sovereignty. Acts 4:27-28 applies this divine sovereignty directly to the conspiracy against Jesus: "they did what your power and will had decided beforehand should happen." The day that is "coming" for the wicked who killed Jesus is the day of judgment and resurrection — already accomplished in the risen Christ.
14: A shadow: "The wicked draw the sword and bend their bow to bring down the poor and helpless, to kill those who walk uprightly." The armed violence of the wicked against the righteous poor is the Psalm's image of injustice at its worst. At the arrest of Jesus, swords and clubs are literally drawn (Matthew 26:47-50). The "poor and helpless" (ʿānî wĕʾebyôn) whom they seek to "bring down" (litpôl) is the vocabulary of the Suffering Servant psalms; Jesus, voluntarily emptied and vulnerable, is the ultimate embodiment of the one targeted by the wicked's violence.
15: A shadow: "Their swords will pierce their own hearts, and their bows will be shattered." The reversal — the weapons of the violent turned against themselves — is the Wisdom pattern of evil self-defeating. At the cross, the apparent victory of the wicked over Jesus became their ultimate defeat: 1 Corinthians 2:8, "None of the rulers of this age understood it, for if they had, they would not have crucified the Lord of glory." The sword turned against the schemers; the cross became the instrument of their judgment, not of Christ's destruction.
16: A revelation of God: "Better is the little that the righteous has than the great wealth of many wicked." The Wisdom reversal of values — the little of the righteous > the abundance of the wicked — is the logic of the Kingdom that Christ announces in the Beatitudes and embodies in his own life. 2 Corinthians 8:9: "For you know the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ, that though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor, so that you through his poverty might become rich." Christ is the paradigmatic "righteous who has little" — and in that poverty makes many rich.
17: A theme: "For the strength of the wicked will be shattered, but the LORD supports the righteous." The divine support of the righteous and the shattering of the wicked's power is the pattern of Christ's resurrection. Hebrews 2:14-15: "by his death he might break the power of him who holds the power of death — that is, the devil." The devil's strength is "shattered" at the cross; the LORD "supports the righteous" by raising Christ from the dead — the definitive demonstration that divine strength upholds those who trust in him.
18: A revelation of God: "The LORD knows the days of the upright, and their inheritance will last forever." The divine knowledge of the righteous person's "days" (yôdēaʿ YHWH yĕmê tĕmîmîm) — and the eternal duration of their inheritance — is the covenant security that grounds resurrection hope. The "forever" (lĕʿôlām) of the inheritance is not merely long duration but the eschatological age beyond death. John 10:28: "I give them eternal life, and they shall never perish; no one will snatch them out of my hand." Christ is the guarantee of the inheritance that is forever.
19: A theme: "They will not be disgraced in days of trouble; in times of famine they will be satisfied." The covenant promise of honor-preservation and sustenance through crisis echoes the manna tradition and the wilderness provision. Christ's feeding of the five thousand (John 6:1-15) and his identity as the "bread of life" (John 6:35: "Whoever comes to me will never go hungry, and whoever believes in me will never be thirsty") fulfill the promise of Psalms 37:19 — the righteous, through union with Christ, are satisfied even in famine.
20: A shadow: "The wicked will perish; the enemies of the LORD will fade like green pastures — they will dissolve, consumed like smoke." The vivid images of fading and dissolving — the enemies of YHWH disappearing like morning mist — are the Psalm's eschatological imagery of judgment. 2 Peter 3:10 uses the same dissolving imagery for the final judgment: "the heavens will disappear with a roar; the elements will be destroyed by fire." The enemies of Christ who opposed him — and who continue to oppose his people — are under this same eschatological sentence.
21: A revelation of God: "The wicked borrows and does not repay, but the righteous is generous and gives." The contrast between covenant economic behavior — the wicked who take but do not return vs. the righteous who give freely — is the character portrait that Christ fulfills perfectly. 2 Corinthians 9:7 ("God loves a cheerful giver") and the entire theology of grace — God giving freely through Christ — reflects the "righteous is generous and gives" of Psalm 37. Christ gives his life freely (John 10:18: "No one takes it from me, but I lay it down of my own accord").
22: A theme: "Those blessed by the LORD will inherit the earth, but those he has cursed will be cut off." The dual eschatological outcome — blessing/inheritance vs. curse/cutting-off — is the judgment framework that Christ applies in the final judgment scene of Matthew 25:34-41: "Come, you who are blessed by my Father; take your inheritance... Depart from me, you who are cursed, into the eternal fire." The "blessed by the LORD who inherit the earth" of Psalm 37 becomes the "blessed by my Father" of Christ's eschatological pronouncement.
23: A type: "The LORD establishes the steps of a man and delights in his way." The divine establishment of the steps of the righteous — YHWH guiding each step and finding delight in the path — is fulfilled in Christ as the one whose steps are perfectly directed. John 8:29: "The one who sent me is with me; he has not left me alone, for I always do what pleases him." The divine delight in the man's way is the divine delight in Jesus ("This is my Son, whom I love; with him I am well pleased," Matthew 3:17) — and through union with Christ, in all who walk in him.
24: A type: "Though he stumbles, he will not fall headlong, for the LORD holds his hand." The divine hand-holding of the righteous who stumble (but do not ultimately fall) is the covenant care imagery. Isaiah 42:6 applies exactly this hand-holding to the Servant: "I, the LORD, have called you in righteousness; I will take hold of your hand." The Servant of Isaiah 42 whom YHWH holds by the hand is the same figure who does not fall permanently even through the deepest valley — Christ stumbling under the cross (Luke 23:26) but not falling, risen by the hand of God.
25: A revelation of God: "I was young and now I am old, yet I have never seen the righteous abandoned or their children begging for food." The experiential testimony of the aged Psalmist — a lifetime of observation confirming that YHWH does not abandon his righteous — grounds resurrection hope. Hebrews 13:5 quotes YHWH's pledge: "Never will I leave you; never will I forsake you." Christ's resurrection is the ultimate vindication of this testimony: God did not abandon the righteous one to the grave (Acts 2:27, citing Psalms 16:10). The righteous are never ultimately abandoned.
26: A revelation of God: "He is always generous and lending, and his children become a blessing." The generosity of the righteous man and the blessing transmitted through his children is the covenant-blessing pattern (Genesis 12:2: "I will bless you... and you will be a blessing"). Christ is the one through whom the Abrahamic blessing flows to "all peoples on earth" (Galatians 3:14: "so that the blessing given to Abraham might come to the Gentiles through Christ Jesus"). The "children" who become a blessing are those who are in Christ — Abraham's offspring (Galatians 3:29).
27: A theme: "Turn away from evil and do good, so you will live in the land permanently." The conditional covenant form — turn, do, live — is the Deuteronomic framework at its most compressed. Christ fulfills this by being the one who permanently "turns away from evil and does good" (Acts 10:38: "he went around doing good") and who thereby secures the "permanent" dwelling — the New Jerusalem, the eternal inheritance. Through him, those who "live in him" (1 John 4:15) receive the permanence that no earthly land-tenure could provide.
28: A revelation of God: "For the LORD loves justice; he does not abandon his faithful people." The divine love of justice (mišpāṭ) as the ground of YHWH's faithfulness to his covenant people is the theological foundation of the atonement. Romans 3:25-26 frames the cross as the demonstration of God's justice: "he left the sins committed beforehand unpunished — he did it to demonstrate his justice at the present time, so as to be just and the one who justifies those who have faith in Jesus." The LORD who loves justice demonstrates that love supremely at the cross of Christ.
29: A theme: "The righteous will inherit the earth and make their home in it forever." The permanent dwelling (yišĕkĕnû lāʿad) of the righteous in the earth/land is the eschatological vision of a renewed creation. Revelation 21:3: "And I heard a loud voice from the throne saying, 'Look! God's dwelling place is now among the people, and he will dwell with them.'" And Psalms 22:5: "they will reign for ever and ever." The "inherit the earth" of Psalm 37 is not replacement of the earth but its renewal — the inheritance Christ secures through the resurrection is the new earth (2 Peter 3:13).
30: A type: "The righteous speak wisdom, and their tongue talks of justice." The wisdom-speech and justice-talk of the righteous man is the profile fulfilled in Christ as the Wisdom of God (1 Corinthians 1:24, 30). Luke 4:22 records that people "spoke well of him and were amazed at the gracious words that came from his lips." His teaching with authority (Matthew 7:28-29), his silencing of his opponents (Matthew 22:46), and his declaration of the final judgment (John 5:27) are the fulfillment of "wisdom" and "justice" from the mouth of the righteous.
31: A type: "The law of his God is in his heart; his steps do not falter." The internalized Torah — law not merely observed but written on the heart — is what Jeremiah 31:33 promises as the New Covenant gift: "I will put my law in their minds and write it on their hearts." Christ is the first and perfect fulfillment of this: the one in whom the law is perfectly internalized, whose steps never falter because Torah is his delight (Psalms 1:2; Matthew 5:17). Through the Spirit, Christ writes this same internalized law on the hearts of those in the New Covenant.
32: A shadow: "The wicked lie in wait for the righteous, looking to kill them." The lethal ambush of the righteous by the wicked is the Psalm's idiom for the opposition to covenant faithfulness. The Gospel narratives are saturated with this imagery: John 5:18 ("the Jewish leaders tried all the more to kill him"), Psalms 7:1 ("the Jewish leaders were looking for a way to kill him"), Psalms 11:53 ("they plotted to take his life"). The righteous of Psalms 37:32 who is ambushed and sought for death is the type whose fullest expression is Christ, hunted by those who hated his righteousness.
33: A revelation of God: "The LORD will not leave the righteous at the mercy of the wicked or let them be condemned at trial." The divine protection from both power and legal condemnation — YHWH intervening in the juridical process — is the resurrection's meaning. Christ was condemned at trial by both Jewish and Roman authority. But YHWH did not "leave him at their mercy" — the resurrection is the divine reversal of the human verdict. Romans 8:33-34: "Who will bring any charge against those whom God has chosen? It is God who justifies. Who then is the one who condemns? No one."
34: A theme: "Wait for the LORD and keep his way; he will exalt you to inherit the earth." The waiting→exaltation→inheritance sequence is the eschatological pattern that Christ undergoes and then mediates. Philippians 2:8-9: "he humbled himself by becoming obedient to death — even death on a cross! Therefore God exalted him to the highest place." Christ waited (Gethsemane), kept the Father's way perfectly (total obedience), and was exalted to inherit "the earth" in the fullest sense — authority over all things (Matthew 28:18: "all authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me").
35: A shadow: "I have seen a ruthless wicked man towering like a flourishing tree in its native ground." The towering wicked man — impressive, rooted, seemingly permanent — is the type of all worldly power that opposes the Messianic kingdom. Daniel 4's great tree cut down (Nebuchadnezzar) and Ezekiel 31's cedar of Lebanon (Pharaoh) use the same image. The Roman Empire, the Jewish establishment, all the powers that crucified Jesus appeared to be "flourishing trees in native ground" — permanent, irresistible.
36: A shadow: "But he passed away — gone; I looked for him and he could not be found." The sudden disappearance of the wicked who seemed permanent — the tree of v35 simply gone — is the Psalm's answer to the temporal flourishing of evil. Revelation 18:10, 17, 19 ("Woe! Woe to you, great city... in one hour your doom has come!") applies this to Babylon, the symbol of all worldly power opposed to Christ. The powers that crucified Jesus have passed away; Christ's kingdom remains (Hebrews 12:28: "we are receiving a kingdom that cannot be shaken").
37: A revelation of God: "Take note of the blameless person and look at the upright; the future (ʾaḥărît) of such a person is peace (šālôm)." The ʾaḥărît — the "end" or "future" of the blameless — is peace/wholeness. This is the Psalm's definitive answer to the theodicy question. Christ is the one who is perfectly "blameless and upright" (tāmîm wĕyāšār) — and his future is demonstrated in the resurrection: Romans 5:1, "since we have been justified through faith, we have peace (eirēnēn) with God through our Lord Jesus Christ." The future of the blameless one is peace — shalom — eternal life.
38: A shadow: "But rebels will all be destroyed together; the future of the wicked will be cut off." The contrast with v37 is stark: the future of rebels is cutting-off, not peace. The ʾaḥărît (future/end) of the wicked is destruction, in direct contrast to the peace of the blameless. Revelation 20:15: "Anyone whose name was not found written in the book of life was thrown into the lake of fire." The "cut off" of Psalms 37:38 is the ultimate language of covenant exclusion — those who reject the blameless Messiah are cut off from the inheritance he mediates.
39: A revelation of God: "The salvation of the righteous comes from the LORD; he is their stronghold in times of trouble." The source of salvation is YHWH alone — the righteous person's security comes not from their own righteousness but from the divine Savior. Acts 4:12: "Salvation is found in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given to mankind by which we must be saved." Christ is the "stronghold in times of trouble" — the name above all names (Philippians 2:9) who is the exclusive locus of the salvation that Psalms 37:39 attributes to YHWH.
40: A revelation of God: "The LORD helps and delivers them; he rescues them from the wicked and saves them, because they take shelter in him." The sequence — help, deliver, rescue, save — all grounded in the single act of sheltering in YHWH — is the comprehensive salvation vocabulary of the OT. 1 Thessalonians 1:10 frames Christ as "Jesus, who rescues us from the coming wrath." The comprehensive salvation work of Psalms 37:40 — performed by YHWH because his people take shelter in him — is performed by Christ and through Christ, in whom the community shelters.
